title: A Pharisee, A Prostitute, and A Prophet
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 04-21-2024 - A Pharisee, A Prostitute, and A Prophet - Luke 7_36-50 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: How Do We Develop a Fervent Love for Jesus?
subpoints: We look at people who are madly in love with Jesus and want to be like them
This passage gives us an example of what that looks like and how to get there

outline.title: The Pharisee: A Cool, Formal, and Distant Religion
subpoints: Simon was a Pharisee — outwardly upright, fasting, praying, tithing, never missing synagogue
He invited Jesus to dinner, likely expecting an interesting theological discussion
Simon had no personal sense of need — he extended no traditional hospitality to Jesus
His relationship with Jesus was formal, distant, and cool — not warm

outline.title: The Prostitute: A Demonstrative, Grateful Love
subpoints: The woman was notorious in the city — a sinner, likely a former prostitute
She must have heard Jesus previously and believed in the gospel before this moment
Jesus's assurances to her in vv. 48 and 50 are words of assurance, not first-time declarations
She risked public humiliation from the very men who had marginalized her
She washed His feet with tears, wiped them with her hair, and anointed them with costly perfume
Her love was not sterile sentiment — this was dirty first-century feet, manure and sewer, and she didn't care

outline.title: The Prophet: Who Is This Man Who Forgives Sins?
subpoints: Simon's thought: if this man were a prophet, he would know what kind of woman is touching him
Luke builds the whole narrative around this question: who is Jesus?
Jesus can only rightly forgive sins done against Himself — which means every sin is ultimately against God
Jesus is not only a prophet but the One whose law this woman had broken — God in human flesh

outline.title: How to Develop Fervent Love for Jesus: The Parable of the Two Debtors
subpoints: We must realize our great debt: all people are sinners (Romans 3:10, 23)
We must realize our utter inability to repay: whether drowning in 50 feet or 500 feet of water, both are drowning
We must trust totally in Christ's grace to forgive us: the creditor forgave both freely (v. 42)
It is faith in Jesus that secures forgiveness, and from that faith fervent love grows

outline.title: Application: Are You Overwhelmed with Sin and Guilt?
subpoints: There is hope for the worst sinners who come to Jesus for forgiveness
If you are overwhelmed with guilt as a believer, either repent of ongoing sin, or stop carrying what Jesus already carried
The best prayer is Mark 9:24: "Lord, I believe; help my unbelief"
Unbelief is what hinders fervent love — faith in Christ produces love for Christ

summary: In this Luke 7:36-50 sermon, Pastor Lyro explores the drama between three characters — a self-righteous Pharisee, a repentant former prostitute, and Jesus — in order to answer the question: how do we develop a fervent love for Jesus? Simon the Pharisee is presented as outwardly religious and upright — fasting, praying, tithing, never missing synagogue — but relating to Jesus with cool formality and no sense of personal need. He extended none of the traditional hospitalities (water for feet, a kiss of greeting, oil for the head) because he had everything together and saw nothing Jesus could offer him of eternal value. The unnamed woman, by contrast, was notorious in the city — likely a former prostitute — who had already heard Jesus and come to faith before that day. She risked the scorn of the very religious men who had marginalized her in order to express her love to her Savior, weeping so much that His feet became wet with her tears, wiping them with her own hair, and anointing them with costly perfume. Pastor Lyro emphasizes the earthy realities of first-century travel: these were feet that had walked through open sewers and animal waste, and she didn't care. Through the parable of the two debtors, Jesus explains the key: the one forgiven much loves much. Both debtors owed more than they could repay — the one in 50 feet of water is just as drowned as the one in 500 feet. Fervent love for Jesus grows from realizing the greatness of our debt, recognizing our utter inability to repay it, and trusting completely in His grace. The sermon closes by noting that Luke's narrative is structured around the question Simon raises: who is this man who forgives sins? Only the One against whom every sin is committed can rightly forgive them — and that is God in human flesh.
transcript:
## Introduction: How Do We Develop a Fervent Love for Jesus?

We look at people who are madly in love with Jesus and we want to be like them. We want to be like those who have this deep relationship with Jesus Christ. How can we get that? How can we become perfectly in love with Jesus? I think this passage gives us an example of that. As we look at these three main characters — the Pharisee, the prostitute, and the prophet — we're going to learn something about being perfectly in love with Jesus Christ.

We're going to read Luke chapter 7, starting at verse 36, and reading through verse 50:

"Then one of the Pharisees asked Him to eat with him. And He went to the Pharisee's house, and sat down to eat. And behold, a woman in the city who was a sinner, when she knew that Jesus sat at the table in the Pharisee's house, brought an alabaster flask of fragrant oil, and stood at His feet behind Him weeping; and she began to wash His feet with her tears, and wiped them with the hair of her head; and she kissed His feet and anointed them with the fragrant oil.

"Now when the Pharisee who had invited Him saw this, he spoke to himself, saying, 'This man, if He were a prophet, would know who and what manner of woman this is who is touching Him, for she is a sinner.'

"And Jesus answered and said to him, 'Simon, I have something to say to you.' So he said, 'Teacher, say it.' 'There was a certain creditor who had two debtors. One owed five hundred denarii, and the other fifty. And when they had nothing with which to repay, he freely forgave them both. Tell me, therefore, which of them will love him more?' Simon answered and said, 'I suppose the one whom he forgave more.' And He said to him, 'You have rightly judged.' Then He turned to the woman and said to Simon, 'Do you see this woman? I entered your house; you gave Me no water for My feet, but she has washed My feet with her tears and wiped them with the hair of her head. You gave Me no kiss, but this woman has not ceased to kiss My feet since the time I came in. You did not anoint My head with oil, but this woman has anointed My feet with fragrant oil. Therefore I say to you, her sins, which are many, are forgiven, for she loved much. But to whom little is forgiven, the same loves little.' Then He said to her, 'Your sins are forgiven.' And those who sat at the table with Him began to say to themselves, 'Who is this who even forgives sins?' Then He said to the woman, 'Your faith has saved you. Go in peace.'"

## The Pharisee: A Cool, Formal, and Distant Religion

Let's start with the Pharisee, Simon. Because Simon was a Pharisee, he probably was a good, upright religious man — at least outwardly. From what we know of being a Pharisee, we can assume that he attempted to keep the system of laws his religious sect had developed. He tithed his income. He fasted regularly — later in Luke 18, the Pharisee in the parable says, "I fast twice a week," which was the prescribed amount: every Monday and Thursday. He likely prayed at least three times every day. He never missed synagogue. He was a decent man, respected as a religious leader in the community.

And as you look at his interaction with Jesus, you see that his relationship with Jesus could be described as formal, distant, and cool — cold, as in cold, not cool as in cool. He invited Jesus to his home, probably thinking that the theological discussion would be interesting. But Simon had no sense of personal need of Jesus. He projected an air of having everything together. He didn't need anybody to save him. He didn't need anybody to forgive him of his sins. He had arrived — he was, after all, a Pharisee, a well-thought-of man in society.

For him, Jesus didn't offer anything of eternal value. He was just a guest. Maybe the conversation would be exciting and he could impress guests by debating with Jesus. But he really wasn't after Jesus. And so there's no anointing of the head, no water to wash the feet, no kissing of welcome — things that would be traditionally extended to a guest invited to your house.

## The Prostitute: A Demonstrative, Grateful Love

Then the scene changes to this woman. Deliberately left unnamed by Luke — perhaps to guard her privacy — she was probably a prostitute. The passage doesn't use that word, but the way it describes her gives the impression. She was notorious in town for her openly sinful way of life. When she entered the room, eyebrows were raised and voices lowered to whispers. Everybody in the room saw this woman the moment she entered.

It took a lot of courage for this woman to seek out Jesus in this gathering — a gathering of the very men who would shun her from town, who would push her to the margins of society. And yet she came there to see Jesus. She knew she would have to endure stares and whispers and muffled laughter. But she wanted openly to express her love for Jesus, and she was willing to endure public humiliation to do that.

Luke doesn't tell us this explicitly, but we must assume that this woman had come to believe in Jesus prior to this time. She had heard Jesus. She had believed in the gospel. Otherwise, she would not have come to do what she did. Jesus's words to her in verses 48 and 50 are words of assurance — not first-time declarations. As this simple woman had heard Jesus speak of the things of God, she sensed that here was a man who did not come to condemn sinners but to save them. This woman was used to being condemned. The Pharisees and society were very good at putting her down — but they never offered her redemption. But Jesus's teaching offered her hope.

When He said, "Come to Me, and I will give you rest," she heard it. When He said, "I have not come to save the righteous but to call sinners to repentance," she heard it. She heard of greedy tax collectors transformed by coming to Jesus. Perhaps she heard of another sinful woman to whom Jesus said, "Neither do I condemn you; go your way and sin no more." She thought: this man offers hope even to a sinner like me. And so she repented of her sins and put her trust in the One who came to seek and save the lost.

Now at these dinners, the guests reclined on couches with their heads near the table, leaning on their left elbow, feet extending away from the table. She planned to slip in and anoint His feet with expensive perfume as He reclined. But when she got there, the love for Christ was too strong. She could not contain her tears. His feet became wet with her weeping. She let her hair down — ignoring the custom that a woman not let her hair down in public — and wiped her Savior's feet with it. The very hair that had been let down in other times for sinful activities was now let down to wipe her Savior's feet.

Let's set emotions aside for a moment and just imagine what she's doing. This is first-century, open-air, sewers everywhere, animals everywhere, walking just wearing sandals — and this woman cannot stop washing and kissing these feet. The feet of a man known for walking everywhere. This is not some sterile little event. This is dirty. This is manure. This is sewer. And yet she is rubbing it with her hair — because at that point she didn't care about herself. She only cared about one person, and that person was Jesus Christ.

In contrast to the cool detachment of the Pharisee, this former prostitute had a servant, demonstrative love for the Lord Jesus who had done so much for her.

Before we look at the third character, let me ask: which of these two — the Pharisee or the prostitute — describes your relationship with Jesus better? Are you more like the cool, calm, and collected Pharisee who has it all together spiritually and doesn't really need what Jesus offers — namely, forgiveness of sins? Or like this woman — do you see that without Jesus you would be hopelessly and helplessly lost in your sins? Are you willing to express your deep feelings of love and gratitude for the Savior in spite of what people might think?

## The Prophet: Who Is This Man Who Forgives Sins?

That brings us to the third character — the prophet. One of Luke's main reasons for relating this story is to get us to reflect on what Simon says in verse 39. Simon's internal critique: if this man were a prophet, he would know what kind of woman is touching him.

Luke builds the whole narrative around that point. Simon thought: if Jesus were a prophet, He would know who and what sort of person this woman is who is touching Him — that she is a sinner. But he ignores the murmuring of the religious crowd, and Christ assures this sinful woman of her forgiveness and sends her away in peace.

You can only rightly forgive sins done against you. You cannot forgive sins done against someone else. And Jesus says, "Your sins are forgiven" — because He knew, and she knew, that every sin committed is against the Lord Jesus Christ. Luke wants us to consider that this man is not only a prophet, but He is also the One whose law this sinful woman had broken — and as God in human flesh, He could rightly forgive sins.

## How to Develop Fervent Love for Jesus

So how do I develop the servant love for Jesus that this sinful woman had — especially if my background is more like that of the Pharisee? Just knowing my personality, I think if I lived in the first century in Israel, I would have been a Pharisee. The black and whiteness, all the rules — I think that's attractive to me. But that's not necessarily attractive to the Lord Jesus Christ.

Jesus answers this question in the story about the two debtors in verses 41 through 43: "There was a certain creditor who had two debtors. One owed five hundred denarii, and the other fifty. And when they had nothing with which to repay, he freely forgave them both. Tell me therefore, which of them will love him more?" Simon answered: "I suppose the one whom he forgave more." And He said: "You have rightly judged."

To love Jesus fervently, we must realize our great debt. In the little story, both parties owe a debt — the greater debtor refers to the sinful woman, the lesser debtor to the Pharisee. But in God's sight, the story is backwards. The woman was not necessarily the greatest sinner — she was the greater debtor, but not necessarily the greater sinner. In his heart, the Pharisee was guilty of pride and self-righteousness, which are serious sins.

Romans 3:10: "There is none righteous, no, not one." Romans 3:23: "For all have sinned and fall short — not fell, but continue to fall short — of the glory of God." So to love Jesus, we must recognize that we owe a great debt to God through Jesus Christ.

To love Jesus fervently, we must also realize our utter inability to repay our debt. Notice that in the story, both debtors cannot pay. Both are over their heads. Which person is in bigger trouble — the one drowning in 50 feet of water, or the one drowning in 500 feet? They are both in the same trouble. They are both drowning and they can't do anything about it. It would be ridiculous for the guy in 50 feet to look at the guy in 500 feet and think, "At least I'm better off than that poor wretch." Yet sinners often think like this. The self-righteous sinner thinks he's better off than the worst sinner — but all the while he's going to drown in his 50 feet.

All the good deeds in the world only add to your sins — it's like putting frosting on a moldy cake. It's still moldy on the inside. You have to come to the place where you recognize your entire cake is moldy, and you cannot do anything to fix it other than starting with a new cake that somebody else gives you.

To love Jesus fervently, you must trust totally in His grace to forgive your unpaid debt of sin. In verse 42, Jesus says: "When they had nothing with which to repay, he freely forgave them both." He didn't look at their character and say they were worthy. He didn't extract a promise to work off the debt. He forgave them graciously and freely. And Jesus states plainly in verse 50: "Your faith has saved you." It is our faith in Jesus Christ that secures our forgiveness. And it is from that faith that fervent love for Christ grows.

## Application

So do you want to grow in fervent love with Jesus? I think the best prayer is that of the father in Mark 9:24: "Lord, I believe; help my unbelief." It is unbelief in our hearts that causes us not to be in fervent love for Jesus Christ. As we grow in our commitment and faith in Jesus Christ, then we grow in love with Him.

Sadly, all that the Pharisees around the table could see was a man who dared to forgive sins. The woman — who once was a prostitute — left in peace because her faith saved her.

Are you overwhelmed with sin and guilt? I hope you can see that there is hope for the very worst of sinners who come to Jesus for forgiveness. He freely forgives both small and large debtors who cast themselves on His mercy. Are you overwhelmed with guilt even though you believe in Jesus Christ? One of two things needs to happen: either you are living in ongoing sin and need to repent and return to faith in Jesus Christ, or you need to stop trying to carry the burden that Jesus already took from you. If you're in Jesus Christ, He has already taken the burden of your guilt. Stop in your pride trying to carry what Jesus has already carried for you.

Sometimes we as Christians are like a child learning to ride a bike whose father let go 100 feet ago, but the child is still screaming, "Don't let go! Don't let go!" — when the father let go long ago. That's how we often are, constantly in guilt for sins that Jesus has already objectively forgiven.

He calls us not to constant guilt but to growing love for Him as we realize we have been forgiven through faith by the sacrifice of Jesus Christ. Let the Lord carry the burden for you.

Father, we thank you that your Son bears our burdens of guilt. Father, we pray that you help us to trust in Him, to grow in faith, that we might grow in love with Him as well. We ask in Jesus' name. Amen.
